note: 50 original question/known-answer pairs used as the spec-7f gold set: the duplicate/leakage guard and a correctness reference for the same domain as card_gen_source.md.

items.q: What kind of molecule are almost all enzymes?
What are catalytic RNA molecules called?
How does an enzyme speed up a reaction?
Does an enzyme change the equilibrium position of a reaction?
Is an enzyme consumed in the reaction it catalyzes?
What is the reactant an enzyme acts on called?
Where on an enzyme does the substrate bind?
Which model says the active site changes shape to fit the substrate?
What is the older model of a rigid enzyme-substrate fit called?
What is the optimal temperature for most human enzymes?
What is the optimal pH for most human enzymes?
At what pH does pepsin work best?
What happens to an enzyme above its optimal temperature?
items.a: It denatures and loses its shape

items.q: Does denaturation break the primary-sequence peptide bonds?
What is a non-protein helper molecule an enzyme needs called?
What is an organic cofactor called?
NAD+ is derived from which vitamin?
What is an enzyme without its cofactor called?
What is the complete active enzyme with its cofactor called?
Give an example of an inorganic cofactor.
items.a: A metal ion such as zinc or magnesium

items.q: Where does a competitive inhibitor bind?
Can adding more substrate overcome a competitive inhibitor?
How does a competitive inhibitor affect Km?
How does a competitive inhibitor affect Vmax?
Where does a noncompetitive inhibitor bind?
items.a: An allosteric site separate from the active site

items.q: How does a noncompetitive inhibitor affect Vmax?
How does a noncompetitive inhibitor affect Km?
What is feedback inhibition?
items.a: When a pathway's end product inhibits an earlier enzyme in that pathway

items.q: What does Km represent?
items.a: The substrate concentration at half of Vmax

items.q: What does a low Km indicate about substrate affinity?
What is Vmax?
items.a: The maximum reaction rate when the enzyme is saturated

items.q: What is the cell's main short-term energy currency?
What does ATP stand for?
What two products form when ATP is hydrolysed?
A reaction that releases free energy is called?
What is the sign of delta G for an exergonic reaction?
A reaction that absorbs free energy is called?
What is the sign of delta G for an endergonic reaction?
How do cells drive endergonic reactions?
items.a: By coupling them to ATP hydrolysis

items.q: Where does glycolysis take place?
What is the net ATP yield of glycolysis per glucose?
What carbon product does glycolysis yield per glucose?
Does glycolysis require oxygen?
Where does the citric acid cycle occur?
Where is the electron transport chain located?
What is the final electron acceptor in the electron transport chain?
Oxygen is reduced to what in the electron transport chain?
What process produces most ATP in aerobic respiration?
What is the proton movement that drives ATP synthesis called?
In anaerobic human muscle, pyruvate is converted to what?
